preamble: The palatalized voiceless alveolar fricative, often arising from an alveolar fricative followed by a palatal approximant, can undergo a historical shift to a voiceless postalveolar fricative. This process, a form of palatalization, involves the tongue moving from an alveolar position with secondary palatal articulation to a primary postalveolar articulation.
phoneticEffects: Palatalization, assimilation, yod-coalescence, fronting of articulation

examples.note: The word 'sure' developed from an earlier pronunciation with /sj/ to /ʃ/ through palatalization.
The word 'sugar' underwent a similar palatalization, with /sj/ becoming /ʃ/.
The suffix '-sion', as in 'mission', historically shifted from /sj/ to /ʃ/ through yod-coalescence.
Iotation of historical *s + *j yields š, seen in the contrast between the infinitive писать 'to write' and the 1sg present пишу 'I write'.
The same *sj > š alternation appears in носить 'to carry' versus the 1sg present ношу 'I carry'.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. Cambridge University Press.
Shevelov, G. Y. (1964). A Prehistory of Slavic: The Historical Phonology of Common Slavic. Carl Winter.
